question: What is a robot?
choices: Any object that uses electricity
A machine that can sense its surroundings, make decisions, and act on them
A toy that looks like a person
A computer program with no physical body
explanation: A robot is a machine built to sense the world around it, decide what to do based on that information, and then act — using motors or other parts to move or do work.

question: Which of these is the best example of a robot?
choices: A hammer
A robot vacuum that drives around and avoids furniture on its own
A pair of scissors
A flashlight
explanation: A robot vacuum senses walls and furniture, decides where to go next, and moves itself — that sense-think-act loop is what makes it a robot, unlike simple tools like a hammer or scissors.

question: Where are robots used in the real world?
choices: Only in science fiction movies
Factories, hospitals, homes, and even outer space
Only inside video games
Only in research labs that nobody can visit
explanation: Robots help build cars in factories, assist surgeons in hospitals, vacuum homes, and explore other planets — they're already part of everyday life.

question: Robotics is best described as a field that combines which subjects?
choices: Only art and music
Mechanical engineering, electronics, and computer programming
Only history and geography
Only cooking and nutrition
explanation: Building a robot means designing its body (mechanical engineering), wiring its circuits (electronics), and writing the instructions it follows (programming) — robotics blends all three.

question: What does it mean when people say a robot is 'automated'?
choices: It performs a task by itself, following a set of instructions, without a person controlling every step
It is painted a bright color
It is the most expensive robot available
It can only work when plugged into a wall outlet
explanation: Automation means the robot follows pre-set instructions to complete a task on its own, instead of a human directly controlling every movement.

question: A remote-controlled toy car that only moves when a person presses the buttons is:
choices: A fully autonomous robot
Not a robot at all — it has no sensors and can't think for itself
An example of artificial intelligence
A self-driving car
explanation: A remote-controlled car has no sensors and makes no decisions of its own — every action comes directly from the human operator, so it doesn't fit the sense-think-act definition of a robot.

question: What is the key difference between a robot and a simple machine like a toaster?
choices: Robots are always bigger than simple machines
A toaster performs the exact same fixed action every time, while a robot can sense conditions and change what it does
Toasters use more electricity than robots
There is no real difference — they are the same thing
explanation: A toaster just heats for a set time no matter what. A robot can use sensors to check its situation (like a vacuum detecting a wall) and adjust its behavior in response.

question: A factory robotic arm that welds car parts in the exact same sequence every time, regardless of its surroundings, is an example of:
choices: A fully intelligent robot that can think like a human
An automated robot following a fixed program, with little or no sensing of its environment
A robot with no program at all
A remote-controlled vehicle
explanation: Many industrial robots repeat the same precise motions over and over based on their program — they may have some sensors for safety, but they aren't adapting their overall plan to a changing environment.

question: Which statement about robots and jobs is most accurate?
choices: Robots can only ever replace human jobs and create nothing new
Robots often take over repetitive or dangerous tasks, while also creating new jobs in designing, building, and maintaining them
Robots are illegal in most workplaces
Robots can only be used for entertainment
explanation: Robots are commonly used for repetitive, dangerous, or precise tasks (like welding or handling hazardous materials), while the robotics industry also creates jobs for engineers, programmers, and technicians.

question: What does 'autonomy' mean for a robot?
choices: How loud the robot is
How much the robot can sense, decide, and act on its own without a human controlling each step
How many colors the robot can be painted
How heavy the robot is
explanation: Autonomy describes how independently a robot can operate — a low-autonomy robot needs constant human control, while a high-autonomy robot can complete tasks with little or no human input.

question: NASA's Mars rovers (like Curiosity and Perseverance) are considered robots mainly because they:
choices: Look like human astronauts
Use sensors to study their surroundings, make some decisions on their own (like avoiding rocks), and move and act using motors and tools
Are remote-controlled every second by someone on Earth
Run only on solar power
explanation: Because radio signals take many minutes to travel between Earth and Mars, the rovers must use onboard sensors and software to make some decisions themselves — combined with movement and tools, that's what makes them robots.

question: Which scenario describes a 'semi-autonomous' robot?
choices: A robot that does absolutely nothing without a human pressing every button for every movement
A robot that can follow a planned path and avoid obstacles on its own, but a human still chooses its destination and can take over if needed
A robot that can never be controlled by a human under any circumstances
A toy that has no sensors or motors at all
explanation: Semi-autonomous robots share control with humans — they can handle some tasks (like obstacle avoidance) independently while a person still provides high-level goals or can step in.

question: What is the most important difference between a robot and a piece of artificial intelligence (AI) software, like a chatbot?
choices: Robots are always smarter than AI software
A robot has a physical body that can sense and act in the physical world, while pure AI software typically only processes information without a body
AI software is always faster than robots
There is no difference between robots and AI software
explanation: AI software (like a chatbot) processes information and produces text or decisions, but it has no body. A robot pairs decision-making with a physical form — sensors, actuators, and a structure — that lets it interact with the real world.

question: A delivery robot rolling on a sidewalk needs to handle an unexpected obstacle, like a fallen branch, that wasn't part of its map. What does this situation best illustrate?
choices: Why robots never need sensors
Why the sense-think-act loop matters: the robot must sense the new obstacle, decide on a new path, and act by steering around it
That the robot should simply stop working forever
That maps are never useful for robots
explanation: Real environments change in ways no map can fully predict. A well-designed robot continuously senses its surroundings, re-evaluates its plan, and acts accordingly — this loop is the foundation of robust robot behavior.

question: Why do engineers often say 'good robot design starts with understanding the task and environment, not just the technology'?
choices: Because the environment and task shape which sensors, actuators, and software a robot actually needs to work well
Because the environment never matters for robot design
Because all robots should be designed identically no matter their job
Because technology should always be chosen before knowing what the robot will do
explanation: A robot that explores the ocean needs very different sensors and materials than one that sorts mail in an office. Understanding the task and environment first helps engineers choose the right combination of sensors, actuators, and software.

question: Which three basic parts work together inside almost every robot?
choices: Sensors, a controller (brain), and actuators
Wheels, paint, and stickers
A screen, a speaker, and a battery only
A keyboard, a mouse, and a monitor
explanation: Sensors gather information, the controller (brain) decides what to do with that information, and actuators carry out the action — together they form the core of any robot.

question: What is the main job of a sensor on a robot?
choices: To move the robot from one place to another
To gather information about the robot or its surroundings, like distance, light, or temperature
To store the robot's power
To make the robot look nicer
explanation: Sensors are the robot's way of gathering information — like eyes, ears, or fingertips — so the controller can know what's happening around it.

question: What is an actuator?
choices: A part that gathers information about the environment
A part that takes action, such as a motor that makes something move
A type of battery
A type of computer screen
explanation: Actuators are the parts that 'do' things — motors that spin wheels, servos that move arms, or other devices that turn decisions into physical action.

question: Which of these is an example of a sensor, not an actuator?
choices: A motor that spins a wheel
A servo that moves a robot arm
An ultrasonic distance sensor that measures how far away a wall is
A speaker that plays a sound
explanation: An ultrasonic distance sensor measures information (distance to an object) — it doesn't move anything itself, which makes it a sensor rather than an actuator.

question: What is often used as the 'brain' of a small robot, like one built with Arduino?
choices: A microcontroller that runs the robot's program and connects to sensors and actuators
A large desktop monitor
A second battery
A piece of cardboard
explanation: A microcontroller is a small computer chip that runs the robot's program, reads data from sensors, and sends signals to actuators — it acts as the robot's brain.

question: Why does a robot need a controller (brain) in addition to sensors and actuators?
choices: It doesn't — sensors and actuators can work fine without any controller
The controller decides what the robot should do based on sensor information, then tells the actuators how to respond
The controller's only job is to make the robot heavier
The controller stores spare parts
explanation: Without a controller, sensor data would have nowhere to go and actuators wouldn't know what to do. The controller is the decision-making link between sensing and acting.

question: A robot bumps gently into a wall and then backs away. Which part most directly detected the wall?
choices: An actuator, like a motor
A sensor, such as a bump (contact) sensor or distance sensor
The robot's battery
The robot's outer paint job
explanation: Detecting the wall is a sensing task — a bump sensor or distance sensor provides that information to the controller, which then commands the motors (actuators) to back away.

question: In robotics, sensors are often called 'inputs' and actuators are often called 'outputs.' Why?
choices: Because sensors and actuators are the same thing with different names
Because sensors send information into the controller (input), while actuators receive commands from the controller and act on them (output)
Because 'input' and 'output' refer only to keyboard and screen devices
Because actuators gather information and sensors perform actions
explanation: Information flows from sensors into the controller (input), gets processed, and then flows out to actuators (output) as commands — this input-process-output pattern is central to how robots work.

question: What would happen to a robot that has working sensors and a working controller, but no actuators?
choices: It would move faster than normal
It could sense and 'think,' but it could not physically move or act in the world
It would become a remote control
Nothing would change — actuators are optional for all robots
explanation: Without actuators, a robot has no way to turn decisions into action — it might process data perfectly but couldn't move a wheel, arm, or any other part.

question: A warehouse robot uses a camera, a distance sensor, and wheel encoders all at once. Why might a robot use multiple sensors instead of just one?
choices: To make the robot look more impressive with no real benefit
Different sensors provide different kinds of information, and combining them gives the controller a more complete and reliable picture
Multiple sensors always cancel each other out and provide no benefit
Robots are required by law to have at least three sensors
explanation: A camera might see a shelf, a distance sensor might detect how close an object is, and wheel encoders track how far the robot has traveled — combining these gives a fuller, more dependable understanding than any one sensor alone.

question: What is the difference between a simple microcontroller (like in an Arduino) and a full computer (like a laptop) when used as a robot's brain?
choices: There is no difference — they are interchangeable in every situation
A microcontroller is typically smaller, cheaper, and good for direct control of motors and sensors, while a full computer offers far more processing power for tasks like image recognition
A microcontroller can run a full desktop operating system, but a laptop cannot
A full computer cannot be connected to any sensors
explanation: Microcontrollers are efficient for reading sensors and driving motors in real time, while full computers (like a Raspberry Pi or laptop) provide the processing power needed for more complex tasks such as analyzing camera images.

question: A line-following robot reads a light sensor, and its controller adjusts the motors so the robot stays on the line. This back-and-forth between sensing and acting is called:
choices: A feedback loop
A power outage
A static design
An open circuit
explanation: A feedback loop is when a robot continuously senses, decides, and acts, then senses again to check the result — the line-following robot constantly checks the line's position and adjusts its motors in response.

question: You're designing a robot to pick ripe strawberries without bruising them. Which combination of parts would be most important to prioritize?
choices: A loud speaker and bright lights, since appearance matters most
Sensors that can detect ripeness and position (like cameras or color sensors), plus gentle actuators (like soft grippers) that can pick fruit without damaging it
The heaviest possible actuators, since more force is always better
No sensors at all, since strawberries don't move
explanation: This task needs sensors that can identify ripe strawberries (color, position) and actuators designed for gentle, precise handling — choosing parts that match the task is a core robotics design skill.

question: A robot's distance sensor reports a value, but the controller's program has a mistake and always tells the motors to drive forward no matter what the sensor says. What is the most accurate description of this problem?
choices: The sensor is broken and needs to be replaced
The actuators are too weak
The sensing works fine, but the controller's decision-making isn't using the sensor data correctly — the sense-think-act loop is broken at the 'think' step
The robot has too many sensors
explanation: If the sensor produces correct readings but the program ignores them, the problem lies in the decision-making logic, not the sensor or the actuator — a good robotics engineer checks each part of the sense-think-act loop separately when debugging.

question: Why might engineers choose a cheaper, less accurate sensor for one robot but a more expensive, highly precise sensor for another robot?
choices: Engineers always pick the cheapest option no matter the task
The choice depends on the task's needs: a toy robot might only need rough information, while a surgical robot needs extremely precise sensors because mistakes could be dangerous
More expensive sensors are always worse than cheap ones
Sensor cost has nothing to do with how a robot is used
explanation: Sensor choice involves trade-offs between cost, precision, size, and power use. A task with high stakes — like surgery — justifies a more precise (and often pricier) sensor, while a toy robot can use simpler, cheaper parts.

question: What gives a robot the energy it needs to move and operate?
choices: Its paint color
A power source, such as a battery
Its weight
The shape of its wheels
explanation: Just like your body needs food for energy, a robot needs a power source — usually a battery — to run its motors, sensors, and controller.

question: What is the main job of a battery in a robot?
choices: To store energy and supply electrical power to the robot's parts
To make the robot's wheels turn directly without any motor
To make decisions for the robot
To detect obstacles
explanation: A battery stores chemical energy and converts it into electrical energy that powers the motors, sensors, and controller.

question: Which part of a robot turns electrical energy into movement?
choices: The battery
A motor
A sensor
The frame
explanation: A motor converts electrical energy from the battery into mechanical energy — spinning a shaft that can turn wheels, gears, or arms.

question: Why do robot builders need to think carefully about battery life?
choices: Battery life doesn't matter for robots
If the battery runs out, the robot can't sense, think, or act anymore — so battery life limits how long a robot can complete its task
Batteries never run out of energy
Robots can run forever without any power source
explanation: Every part of a robot — sensors, motors, and the controller — draws power from the battery. Once it's drained, the robot stops working, so engineers plan for how long a task will take versus how much charge the battery holds.

question: A robot with solar panels on top is an example of:
choices: A robot that gets some or all of its power from sunlight instead of (or in addition to) a battery
A robot that has no power source at all
A robot that runs only at night
A robot that doesn't need any energy to work
explanation: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity, which can charge a battery or power the robot directly — useful for robots that operate outdoors for long periods, like some Mars rovers.

question: What is the main difference between a rechargeable battery and a disposable (single-use) battery?
choices: Rechargeable batteries can be recharged and used many times, while disposable batteries are thrown away once empty
Disposable batteries can be recharged hundreds of times, but rechargeable batteries cannot
There is no difference between the two types
Rechargeable batteries never run out of power
explanation: Rechargeable batteries (like lithium-ion) can be plugged in and refilled with energy many times, which is why most robots use them — replacing disposable batteries constantly would be wasteful and expensive.

question: What typically happens to a mobile robot's behavior as its battery runs low?
choices: The robot speeds up and becomes more accurate
The robot may slow down, lose power to its motors, or shut down entirely as voltage drops
The robot's sensors automatically become more powerful
Nothing changes until the battery is at exactly 0%
explanation: As a battery discharges, the voltage it can supply often drops, which can cause motors to run weaker or sensors to behave unreliably — many robots are programmed to return to a charging station before the battery gets too low.

question: A robot designer wants to add a much bigger battery to make a drone fly longer. What trade-off should they consider?
choices: Bigger batteries have no downsides at all
A bigger battery adds weight, which can make the drone less agile and require more energy just to lift the extra battery itself
Bigger batteries always make a robot lighter
Battery size never affects a flying robot's performance
explanation: Every gram of extra battery is extra weight the motors must lift and move. Engineers balance battery size (more energy stored) against the added weight and its effect on performance.

question: Why are small DC (direct current) motors so commonly used in beginner robotics kits?
choices: They are simple, affordable, and convert battery power directly into rotational motion for wheels or gears
They cannot be powered by batteries
They only work with solar panels
They require no electricity to operate
explanation: DC motors are inexpensive, easy to control with simple circuits, and run directly off battery power — making them a go-to choice for beginner robots and hobby projects.

question: Why is 'energy efficiency' especially important for a mobile robot, like a delivery robot or a Mars rover?
choices: Efficiency doesn't matter if a robot has wheels
A more efficient robot can do more work, travel farther, or operate longer on the same amount of stored energy
Efficient robots are always slower and less useful
Energy efficiency only matters for robots that never move
explanation: Since mobile robots can't always plug into a wall, every bit of energy they save (through efficient motors, lightweight design, or smart power management) extends how long they can operate before recharging or running out.

question: In electricity, what do voltage and current together help determine?
choices: The color of the robot
The amount of electrical power available to run the robot's motors and electronics
How many sensors a robot can have
The robot's weight
explanation: Electrical power is related to both voltage (the 'push' behind the electricity) and current (the flow of electricity) — together they determine how much power is delivered to the robot's parts.

question: A robot designed to explore deep space for years, far from sunlight, would most likely need:
choices: Only a small AA battery
A long-lasting power source not dependent on sunlight, such as a radioisotope thermoelectric generator (RTG), which some NASA spacecraft use
No power source — space robots don't need energy
A power cord plugged into a wall on Earth
explanation: Far from the Sun, solar panels become much less effective. Some NASA missions use RTGs, which generate steady electrical power from the heat of decaying radioactive material, providing reliable energy for years.

question: A robotics team is designing a robot that must operate continuously for 8 hours on a single charge while carrying heavy equipment. Which combination of choices would best help meet this goal?
choices: Choose the heaviest possible battery and the least efficient motors available
Balance battery capacity against weight, choose efficient motors, and write software that avoids wasting energy (like turning off unused sensors)
Remove the battery entirely to save weight
Add as many extra sensors as possible regardless of power use
explanation: Meeting a long runtime goal usually requires balancing multiple factors at once: enough battery capacity, motors and components chosen for efficiency, and software that manages power smartly — for example, by powering down sensors that aren't currently needed.

question: Some electric vehicles and robots use 'regenerative braking,' where slowing down recovers some energy back into the battery. What does this best illustrate?
choices: That braking always wastes 100% of a robot's energy with no way to recover any of it
That clever engineering can recapture some energy that would otherwise be lost, improving overall efficiency
That batteries can charge themselves with no input energy at all
That motors and brakes are unrelated to energy use
explanation: When slowing down, a motor can be run in reverse as a generator, converting some of the robot's motion back into electrical energy stored in the battery — a clever way to reduce overall energy waste.

question: Mars rovers like Curiosity and Perseverance use RTGs instead of solar panels (unlike earlier rovers). What is the most likely reason for this design choice?
choices: RTGs are lighter than solar panels in every case
RTGs provide steady power day and night and aren't affected by dust storms that can cover solar panels, which matters for a long, ambitious mission
Solar panels don't work anywhere in the solar system
RTGs are simply more colorful than solar panels
explanation: Mars experiences dust storms that can block sunlight for weeks and long nights with no sun at all. An RTG provides consistent power regardless of weather or time of day, which is valuable for missions designed to last many years.

question: What is a simple machine?
choices: A basic device, like a lever or wheel, that makes a task easier by changing the size or direction of a force
A robot with a computer chip inside
Any machine that runs on batteries
A machine that can only be used once
explanation: Simple machines — like levers, wheels, ramps, and gears — are basic tools that change the size or direction of a force, making tasks like lifting or moving easier.

question: Which of these is an example of a lever?
choices: A seesaw, which pivots to lift weight on one side when you push down on the other
A flashlight beam
A puddle of water
A piece of string with nothing attached
explanation: A seesaw is a classic lever: it pivots around a fixed point (the fulcrum), so pushing down on one end lifts the other end — the same idea used in tools like crowbars and robotic arms.

question: What does a wheel and axle help a robot do?
choices: Sense light levels
Move more easily by rolling instead of dragging along the ground
Store electrical energy
Send wireless signals
explanation: A wheel turning around an axle reduces friction compared to dragging an object, letting a robot move with much less effort.

question: What is the main purpose of gears in a robot?
choices: To make the robot look more colorful
To transfer motion and force between parts, often changing speed or direction in the process
To store data
To detect light
explanation: Gears are toothed wheels that mesh together to pass motion from one part to another — and depending on their sizes, they can speed up, slow down, or redirect that motion.

question: Why might a robot designer choose legs instead of wheels for a robot that needs to climb stairs?
choices: Wheels are always better than legs in every situation
Legs can step up and over obstacles like stairs, which wheels often struggle to climb
Legs use no energy at all
Stairs are easier for wheeled robots than for legged robots
explanation: Wheels are great on flat, smooth ground, but legs can lift over gaps and steps — which is why some robots (and animals!) use legs to handle rough or stepped terrain.

question: What is friction, and how does it relate to a robot's wheels?
choices: Friction is a type of electrical signal used by sensors
Friction is a force that resists motion between surfaces — robot wheels rely on some friction with the ground to grip and move without slipping
Friction only exists in outer space
Friction makes wheels spin faster with no extra effort
explanation: Friction between the wheel and the ground is what allows the wheel to push against the surface and move the robot forward — too little friction and the wheels spin in place (slipping).

question: In a gear system, if a small gear with few teeth drives a much larger gear with many teeth, what generally happens?
choices: The large gear spins faster than the small gear, with less force
The large gear spins slower than the small gear, but with more turning force (torque)
Both gears always spin at exactly the same speed and force
Gears have no effect on speed or force at all
explanation: When a small gear drives a larger one, the larger gear turns more slowly but with greater torque — this trade-off between speed and force is a key idea in gear systems.

question: A long pry bar lets you lift a heavy rock with less effort than using your hands directly. This is an example of:
choices: Electrical resistance
Mechanical advantage from a lever — the bar multiplies the force you apply
A computer algorithm
A type of sensor
explanation: A lever provides 'mechanical advantage': by pushing down on a long bar with a pivot point near the rock, a small force from you creates a much larger force on the rock.

question: Why might a robot use a ramp (inclined plane) to move a heavy object to a higher shelf instead of lifting it straight up?
choices: Ramps make objects heavier
A ramp lets the robot apply a smaller force over a longer distance instead of a larger force straight up — making the task easier, even though it takes more movement
Ramps remove the need for any force at all
Ramps only work for robots with wings
explanation: An inclined plane spreads the work of lifting over a longer path, reducing the force needed at any one moment — a classic simple-machine trade-off between force and distance.

question: A robot designer is choosing a gear ratio for a robot's wheels. What trade-off are they balancing?
choices: There is no trade-off — higher gear ratios always make a robot better in every way
Higher gear ratios tend to increase torque (useful for climbing or carrying loads) but reduce top speed, while lower ratios do the opposite
Gear ratios only affect the robot's color
Gear ratios determine how many sensors a robot can have
explanation: Gear ratios let designers trade speed for torque (or vice versa). A robot that needs to climb ramps or carry heavy loads benefits from higher torque, even if it moves more slowly — while a racing robot might prioritize speed.

question: Many real robots combine multiple simple machines — for example, a robotic arm might use levers, gears, and wheels together. What is this combination called?
choices: A perpetual motion machine
A compound machine — two or more simple machines working together to perform a task
A simple circuit
An impossible machine
explanation: A compound machine combines simple machines (like levers and gears) to achieve more complex movements — most real robotic mechanisms are compound machines.

question: A robot's wheels are spinning rapidly but the robot isn't moving forward. What is the most likely explanation?
choices: The robot has too much friction between its wheels and the ground
The wheels are slipping because there isn't enough friction (grip) with the surface to convert spinning into forward motion
The robot's battery is too full
The robot has too many sensors
explanation: If there's too little friction — like on ice or a very smooth floor — the wheels can spin freely without gripping the surface, so the robot doesn't move forward even though the motors are working.

question: A robot's motor spins at 100 rotations per minute (RPM). It drives a gear system with a 4:1 ratio (the output gear turns once for every 4 turns of the motor gear). What is the approximate output speed, and what happens to torque?
choices: Output speed is about 400 RPM, and torque decreases
Output speed is about 25 RPM, and torque increases
Output speed is still 100 RPM, and torque stays the same
Output speed is about 25 RPM, and torque also decreases
explanation: A 4:1 gear ratio divides the speed by 4 (100 RPM → 25 RPM) while roughly multiplying the torque by 4 — gear systems trade speed for torque, not gain both for free.

question: Two teams build robots for a competition: one robot must climb a steep ramp carrying a heavy load, and the other must race across a flat track as fast as possible. How should their gear choices most likely differ?
choices: Both robots should use identical gear ratios since gear choice never matters for competitions
The climbing robot should use a gear ratio favoring higher torque (slower but stronger), while the racing robot should favor higher speed (less torque)
The racing robot should use the highest possible torque and lowest possible speed
Gear ratios only matter for robots with cameras
explanation: Climbing a steep ramp with a load requires more torque to overcome gravity and resistance, even at the cost of speed. A flat-track racer benefits more from higher speed since it doesn't need to fight gravity as much — different goals call for different gear ratios.

question: A robotic gripper uses a lever arm so that a small motor near the base can apply a strong gripping force at the fingertips, far from the pivot. What principle explains why this works?
choices: Levers provide mechanical advantage: applying force over a longer arm near the pivot can produce a larger force at another point, depending on the lengths involved
Motors near the base are always weaker than motors at the fingertips
Lever arms have no effect on the force at the fingertips
This only works if the gripper has no pivot point at all
explanation: By placing the motor near the pivot and using lever arms of carefully chosen lengths, engineers can amplify a modest motor force into a stronger gripping force at the fingertips — the same lever principle as a pair of pliers.
